topic.what: Maastricht V/Florence Consensus Report providing updated European evidence-based guidelines for H. pylori infection management, covering indications for treatment, diagnostic strategies, first- and second-line therapies, prevention of gastric cancer, and special populations.
topic.how: Delphi-method consensus among 43 international experts across five working groups, with systematic literature review and GRADE-quality evidence grading for each recommendation.
topic.why_matters: The European benchmark guideline that standardized H. pylori management across the continent, establishing treat-all-infected as default policy and formally positioning H. pylori eradication as gastric cancer prevention.

characteristics.value: Five working groups: indications/associations, diagnostics, treatment, prevention, H. pylori and gastric microbiota
characteristics.vs_baseline: Expanded from Maastricht IV with new cancer prevention and microbiota sections
characteristics.context: European Helicobacter and Microbiota Study Group

characteristics.what_it_measures: Level of recommendation for treating all H. pylori-positive individuals
characteristics.value: H. pylori gastritis is an infectious disease — all infected patients should be offered treatment
characteristics.vs_baseline: Previous versions had more conditional treatment indications
characteristics.evidence: Consensus statement with strong recommendation, high-quality evidence
characteristics.context: Applies to all H. pylori-positive individuals unless competing considerations exist

good_for: Standardizing H. pylori management in Europe
not_for: Regions with very different resistance patterns (e.g., Southeast Asia)
requires: Local antibiotic resistance data for therapy selection
Diagnostic infrastructure (UBT, stool antigen, or endoscopy)

novelty: Formally classified H. pylori gastritis as an infectious disease regardless of symptoms
Recommended treating all infected individuals as default policy
Integrated gastric cancer prevention as a primary eradication indication
Added gastric microbiota section acknowledging broader microbial ecology
open_problems: Rising antibiotic resistance may outpace guideline update cycles
Implementation gap between guideline recommendations and clinical practice
Optimal population-level screening strategy remains debated
Long-term microbiome consequences of mass eradication insufficiently studied
